coredata.dc:title: Orange/red dual-emissive boron- and nitrogen-codoped carbon dots for wash-free and selective staining of lipid droplets in live cells
coredata.dc:creator: She, Cong
Wang, Zihao
Zeng, Jia
Wu, Fu-Gen
coredata.dc:description: Lipid droplet (LD) is an organelle that stores lipids and plays crucial roles in many metabolic processes and diseases. However, developing a method for long-term tracing of LDs and monitoring LD movement remains a challenge. Here, by using a solvent-free carbonization method, we prepare a kind of boron- and nitrogen-codoped carbon dots (BNCDs) from 3-aminophenylboronic acid. The as-synthesized BNCDs have an average size of ∼6.9 nm, long-wavelength fluorescence emission, and excitation wavelength-independent fluorescence emission, and can realize wash-free LD imaging without the introduction of LD-targeting ligands. Besides, the BNCDs have the characteristics of good biocompatibility, simple preparation, and low cost. More importantly, the BNCDs can effectively track the dynamic changes of LDs (including lipophagy) in live animal cells. This work shows that the developed BNCDs can be used as an ideal probe for nondestructively visualizing LDs, which may have implications for the future studies on LDs-related metabolism and diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
LD staining
Dry heating
Excitation wavelength-independent
Lipophagy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Pluronic F-127 (PF127)/BNCDs and DPO/BNCDs
Optical properties and cytotoxicity of BNCDs
Selective staining of LDs using BNCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of BNCDs and their applications for selective LD staining. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Characterization of BNCDs. (a) TEM image and (b) corresponding size distribution histogram. (c) FTIR spectrum. (d) Full-range XPS curve. (e–h) High-resolution XPS curves of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and B 1s, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence excitation (Ex)/emission (Em) spectra of BNCDs in DMSO. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of BNCDs collected at different excitation wavelengths in DMSO. (c and d) Fluorescence stability evaluation results of BNCDs (dispersed in ethanol) under storage at room temperature or irradiated by 532 nm light for different time periods. (e and f) Relative viabilities of A549 and HMEC-1 cells treated with BNCDs at different doses for 24 h. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

CLSM images of BNCDs (20 μg/mL)-incubated A549 cells costained with BODIPY 493/503 (1 μg/mL), LysoTracker Green (1 μM), ER-Tracker Green (1 μM), or Rhod 123 (0.5 μM). Shown in the last column are the intensity correlation plots of BNCDs and BODIPY 493/503/LysoTracker Green/ER-Tracker Green/Rhod 123. Scale bars: 25 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

CLSM images of LD localization in different cells (Hep G2, 4T1, L02, and HMEC-1). Before imaging, the cells were stained with BNCDs (20 μg/mL) and BODIPY 493/503 (1 μg/mL) for 15 min. Shown in the last column are the intensity correlation plots of BNCDs and BODIPY 493/503. Scale bars: 25 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) CLSM images of the A549 cells treated with BNCDs only (control) or treated with BNCDs and 4 °C/one of the endocytosis inhibitors (MβCD, genistein, CPZ, and amiloride), and (b) corresponding normalized FL intensities. Scale bar: 25 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematic illustration of the fabrication of PF127/BNCDs and DPO/BNCDs. (b) CLSM images of the A549 cells treated with PF127/BNCDs or DPO/BNCDs. Scale bars: 25 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

CLSM images recorded for tracking the dynamic position changes of LDs in two representative A549 cells by BNCDs (20 μg/mL) at 37 °C on a stage top incubator. Four pseudo-colors indicate the moving trajectory of LDs at different time points (0–6 min). Scale bars: 10 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
CLSM images of the A549 cells stained by BNCDs (20 μg/mL) and LysoTracker Green (1 μM) for different time periods. Scale bars: 25 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Orange/red dual-emissive boron- and nitrogen-codoped carbon dots for wash-free and selective staining of lipid droplets in live cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, 2 Sipailou Road, Nanjing, 210096, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, 2 Sipailou Road, Nanjing, 210096, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: C.S. and Z.W. contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lipid droplet (LD) is an organelle that stores lipids and plays crucial roles in many metabolic processes and diseases. However, developing a method for long-term tracing of LDs and monitoring LD movement remains a challenge. Here, by using a solvent-free carbonization method, we prepare a kind of boron- and nitrogen-codoped carbon dots (BNCDs) from 3-aminophenylboronic acid. The as-synthesized BNCDs have an average size of ∼6.9 nm, long-wavelength fluorescence emission, and excitation wavelength-independent fluorescence emission, and can realize wash-free LD imaging without the introduction of LD-targeting ligands. Besides, the BNCDs have the characteristics of good biocompatibility, simple preparation, and low cost. More importantly, the BNCDs can effectively track the dynamic changes of LDs (including lipophagy) in live animal cells. This work shows that the developed BNCDs can be used as an ideal probe for nondestructively visualizing LDs, which may have implications for the future studies on LDs-related metabolism and diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: Lipid droplet (LD), a main cellular organelle for lipid storage, consists of a lipophilic core and a phospholipid monolayer shell [1]. As a highly dynamic organelle for intracellular lipid storage and trafficking, LDs can interact with other subcellular compartments like endoplasmic reticulum, mitochondrion, and lysosome to maintain physiological homeostasis [2]. Thus, LDs are associated with many biological activities, such as membrane transport, lipid metabolism, protein degradation, and signal transduction [3]. In addition, the formation of LDs is also related to many diseases, such as atherosclerosis, inflammation, and cancer [4]. Therefore, it is crucial to track LD movement in live cells. The fluorescence imaging technology with the advantages of easy operation, little destruction, and real-time detection provides an ideal approach to visualize subcellular structures. A variety of dyes have been designed and used for LD monitoring, including BODIPY 493/503, Nile Red, and Oil Red O [5]. However, these dyes require complex organic synthesis and are expensive, and have many shortcomings—For BODIPY 493/503, it has a small Stokes shift, which may cause self-absorption; for Nile Red, it has poor LD-binding specificity and may stain other organelles; for Oil Red O, it can only stain the fixed cells and cannot be monitored dynamically, which seriously hinders their application in live cell imaging [6]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop new LD fluorescent probes with simple preparation, large Stokes shift, high LD staining selectivity, and dynamic monitoring capacity for studying the number, morphology, and dynamic movement of LDs.

In recent years, many LD-targeting fluorescent probes including aggregation-induced emission-based fluorescent probes [5,7,8], intramolecular charge transfer-based fluorescent probes [9–11], coumarin-based fluorescent probes [12], single-benzene-based fluorescent probe [13], and fluorogenic merocyanine fluorophores [14] have been developed. However, many of these LD probes have limitations like the requirements of complicated organic synthesis and extra introduction of LD-targeting ligands, poor LD-targeting performance, and/or inability to achieve dynamic LD tracking. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop simple synthetic approaches to fabricate new fluorescent probes possessing the intrinsic capacity of realizing high-quality imaging and dynamic tracking of intracellular LDs. In this regard, developing one-step synthetic methods of the fluorescent probes with the ability to effectively target LDs are urgently needed.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials with a size generally less than 10 nm [15]. CDs have attracted much attention in recent years for their excellent properties, such as small size, low cost, high biocompatibility, and excellent optical properties. Due to these attractive properties, CDs have been widely used in bioimaging, biosensing, catalysis, ion detection, anti-counterfeiting, antibacterial, and drug delivery applications [16–32]. To design and fabricate CDs with different properties/functions, heteroatom (such as nitrogen, sulfur, and boron) doping is an efficient approach. For instance, Liu et al. used CDs and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) to prepare flexible solid films with dynamic ultralong room temperature phosphorescence [33]. Kang et al. synthesized N,S-doped CDs by microwave heating and investigated the relationship between the antibacterial activity of the CDs and the proportion of doping elements [34]. Rong et al. obtained B,N-doped CDs by a combustion method and developed a Cu2+ probe via a static fluorescence quenching mechanism [35]. However, many of the developed CDs show blue or green fluorescence under the excitation of ultraviolet/visible light, which hinders their biomedical applications because short-wavelength excitation light is harmful to living cells. Besides, the applications of most reported CDs have excitation-dependent emission characteristic which may cause fluorescence overlap with other fluorescent probes [36–43]. Furthermore, only limited types of CDs can target subcellular organelles in cells. For example, Geng et al. prepared mitochondria-targeting CDs by retrosynthesis for long-term mitochondrial tracking [44]. Li et al. developed l-cysteine-rich chiral carbon quantum dots for long-term in situ imaging of the Golgi apparatus and the carbon quantum dots could be used as a precise ricin A-chain (RTA) transporter for targeted delivery to the Golgi apparatus [45,46]. In our previous work, we developed several types of fluorescent CDs for mitochondrion, lysosome, and nucleolus imaging [47–51]. Regarding CDs for LD staining, there are only very limited reports up till now [52–55]. For example, Liu et al. prepared two LD-targeting green-emitting CDs by the treatment of o-phenylenediamine (oPD) with nitric acid at room temperature for 4 h and hydrothermal treatment of oPD at 200 °C for 10 h, respectively [52]. Besides, Gao et al. fabricated yellow-emitting CDs by solvothermal treatment of oPD and thiourea at 210 °C for 10 h, followed by the further purification using silica gel column chromatography, and the obtained CDs could be used as a fluorescent probe for selective imaging of LDs in HepG2 cells, mouse liver tissues, and live zebrafish [53]. In addition, Gao et al. synthesized a kind of green-emitting CDs containing a well-defined fluorophore (2-dimethylamino-5-fluorobenzimidazole) by solvothermal treatment of 1,2-diamino-4-fluorobenzene and thiourea, followed by the further silica gel column chromatography purification [54]. The authors revealed that the CDs could realize in situ wash-free imaging of LDs and epidermal cell membranes in live zebrafish. In another report, Wang et al. prepared LD-targeting red-emitting CDs by solvothermal treatment of 4-piperidinoaniline (a p-phenylenediamine derivative), followed by the purification by silica gel column chromatography [55]. The authors successfully applied the CDs for visualizing the dynamic behavior of LDs and monitoring the bisphenol A-caused nonalcoholic fatty liver disease-like changes of lipid metabolism. Although these previous studies have realized desirable LD tracking via CDs, some problems like short emission wavelength, nonspecific LD targeting, and/or complicated preparation/purification steps of the CDs still exist, which makes it necessary to develop simple and low-cost preparation/purification approaches of fluorescent CDs with long wavelength emission and high-quality LD staining properties.

Here, we designed orange/red dual-emissive boron (B)- and nitrogen (N)-codoped carbon dots (BNCDs) by using 3-aminophenylboronic acid as the raw material through a solvent-free carbonization (dry heating) method. The synthesis process is simple, reproducible, and suitable for massive production. In addition, the BNCDs display a large Stokes shift and excitation-independent emission property. More importantly, the BNCDs have an intrinsic capacity of realizing excellent LD-specific imaging without the additional modification with LD-targeting ligands and can be used for wash-free and real-time tracking of the LDs in live cells. We also demonstrated the lipophagy monitoring capability and excellent biocompatibility of the as-synthesized BNCDs.

The BNCDs were prepared via an in situ solvent-free carbonization (dry heating) method: 1 g 3-aminophenylboronic acid was added to a 10 mL teflon-lined autoclave, which was sealed and maintained at a temperature of 220 °C for 20 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained powder was washed with deionized (DI) water via suction filtration for more than three times to remove impurities, and the remnant was dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Pluronic F-127 (PF127)/BNCDs and DPO/BNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: 20 mg PF127 or 1,2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-[methoxy(polyethylene glycol)-2000] (ammonium salt) (DSPE-PEG2000-OMe) and 2 mg BNCDs were dissolved together in 0.5 mL dichloromethane in a 4 mL centrifuge tube and dried under a stream of nitrogen. Then the above-obtained samples were thoroughly dried under vacuum at room temperature for more than 1 h, and 1 mL of DI water was added to each centrifuge and the resulting suspensions were sonicated at 25 °C for 30 min, yielding the final PF127/BNCD and DPO/BNCD aqueous dispersions. The products were stored in a refrigerator at ∼4 °C.

Materials and other experimental details including characterization of BNCDs, measurement of photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), fluorescence stability evaluation of BNCDs under different conditions, effect of solvent polarity on the fluorescence emission of BNCDs, cell culture, cytotoxicity assay, time-dependent confocal imaging and flow cytometry analysis of A549 cells (a human lung cancer cell line) after treatment with BNCDs, cell imaging, endocytosis mechanism assay, dynamic monitoring of LDs using BNCDs, and lipophagy evaluation assay are given in supporting information.

As depicted in Fig. 1 , the BNCDs were prepared by heating the 3-aminophenylboronic acid dry powder at 220 °C for 20 h. After purification with water and drying, the brown solid product was obtained. As revealed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), the BNCDs (dispersion solvent: ethanol) showed a uniform size distribution with an average diameter of 6.9 ± 1.8 nm (Fig. 2 a and b). Besides, the atomic force microscopy (AFM) image displayed in Fig. S1 also revealed that the BNCDs (dipersion solvent: ethanol) were highly dispersed with an average height of ∼9.5 nm. The high-resolution TEM result demonstrated that the CDs did not have crystalline structures (Fig. S2). We also characterized the BNCDs using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2c, the characteristic peaks of BNCDs were at 3218, 1467, and 1196 cm−1, which were attributed to O–H, CC (or B–O), and B–O–H (or C–O), respectively, and these peaks also appeared in the FTIR spectrum of 3-aminophenylboronic acid (Fig. S3). The XPS curves showed the presence of C, N, O, and B elements with the corresponding atomic percentages of 16.2%, 1.2%, 45.6%, and 37.0% (Fig. 2d). The high-resolution curve of C 1s shown in Fig. 2e exhibited four peaks at 283.9, 284.6, 285.8, and 288.5 eV, which can be attributed to C–B, C–C/CC, C–O/C–N, and CO/CN, respectively. The three peaks at 399.4, 400.7, and 402.0 eV in the N 1s high-resolution curve shown in Fig. 2f were attributed to pyridinic N, amino N, and pyrrolic N, respectively. In the O 1s high-resolution curve shown in Fig. 2g, the peaks at 533.2 and 534.3 eV correspond to B–O and C–O/C=O, respectively. In addition, two peaks at 193.1 and 194.0 eV can be observed in the B 1s high-resolution curve (Fig. 2h), which can be ascribed to the BCO2 and B–O, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and cytotoxicity of BNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the BNCDs were investigated via ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectroscopic techniques. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the absorption bands at around 526 and 566 nm can be assigned to the n−π∗ transition of the aromatic sp2 system containing CO and CN bonds. The PLQY of BNCDs (in ethanol) was measured to be 15.2% using rhodamine B as the reference. The fluorescence emission of BNCDs in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) showed two obvious peaks at 575 and 620 nm with a shoulder peak at 675 nm under 526 nm excitation, in which the orange/red dual emission could be ascribed to the high graphitization degree [56–58] and oxidation degree [59,60] as well as the different emission centers [61–63] of BNCDs. Fig. 3b showed that the fluorescence emission of the BNCDs was excitation-independent under excitation from 460 to 580 nm. As shown in Fig. 3c and d, the fluorescence stability of the BNCDs dispersed in ethanol under room temperature storage and 532 nm light irradiation was investigated, and the results indicated the good photostability of the BNCDs under these conditions. Moreover, we recorded the fluorescence emission spectra of the BNCDs dispersed in different solvents with different polarities (i.e., acetone, DMSO, N,N-dimethylformamide, ethanol, methanol, and H2O). The corresponding results in Fig. S4 clearly showed that the fluorescence emission intensity of BNCDs was sharply enhanced in the less polar organic solvents compared with H2O, indicating that the BNCDs can be used as a wash-free fluorescent probe for organelle imaging, since intracellular organelles may have similar less polar environment like the organic solvents.

We then adopted 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay to evaluate the cytocompatibility of BNCDs before the cellular imaging experiments. As displayed in Fig. 3e and f, the MTT results showed that the viabilities of A549 and HMEC-1 (a human microvascular endothelial cell line) cells could maintain over 90% after 24 h incubation with the BNCDs even at a concentration of 200 μg/mL. This result confirmed the excellent biocompatibility of BNCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective staining of LDs using BNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: As presented in Fig. S5, bright red emission could be observed under 552 nm excitation for BNCDs-treated A549 cells. And the cells still maintained strong fluorescence emission after 24 h. This indicated the potential of BNCDs for long-time cellular imaging. Moreover, flow cytometric results showed that BNCDs could be rapidly endocytosed by the cells and the cellular fluorescence reached a plateau at 2 h (Fig. S6).

To demonstrate the LD tracking ability of BNCDs, we treated A549 cells with BNCDs and each of the following organelle-targeting fluorescent probes: BODIPY 493/503 (targeting LD), LysoTracker Green (targeting lysosome), ER-Tracker Green (targeting endoplasmic reticulum, ER), and rhodamine 123 (Rhod 123; targeting mitochondrion). As shown in Fig. 4 , the merged confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images clearly indicated the good colocalization of BNCDs and BODIPY 493/503. Moreover, PCC (Pearson's correlation coefficient) and OLC (overlap coefficient) values of the BNCDs–BODIPY 493/503 group (0.90 and 0.90, respectively) were much higher than those of the BNCDs–LysoTracker Green group (0.14 and 0.17, respectively), the BNCDs–ER-Tracker Green group (0.10 and 0.13, respectively), and the BNCDs–Rhod 123 group (0.11 and 0.13, respectively), proving that BNCDs have specific staining capacity for LDs.

To evaluate the staining effect of BNCDs in different cells, we selected four other types of cells (human hepatocellular carcinoma cell Hep G2, murine breast cancer cell 4T1, human embryo liver cell L02, and HMEC-1) to repeat the above experiments. As shown in Fig. 5 , the BNCDs-stained red dots well overlaps with the BODIPY 493/503-stained green dots in the four types of cells, indicating that the BNCDs have the potential to be used as a universal LD probe for various types of cells.

In addition, we investigated the cellular uptake mechanism of BNCDs via endocytosis inhibition experiments. In general, there are four common types of endocytosis pathways (i.e., macropinocytosis, clathrin-mediated endocytosis (CME), caveolae-mediated endocytosis (CvME), and lipid raft-mediated endocytosis (LrME)). Accordingly, we evaluated the influences of 5-(N,N-dimethyl)-amiloride hydrochloride (amiloride, inhibitor of macropinocytosis), chlorpromazine (CPZ, inhibitor of CME), genistein (inhibitor of CvME), and methyl-β-cyclodextrin (MβCD, inhibitor of LrME) on the cellular uptake amounts of BNCDs. Besides, we also used 4 °C treatment to check if the endocytosis was energy-dependent due to the fact that 4 °C treatment can reduce the enzyme activities to lower the generation amount of ATP. The CLSM images and flow cytometric results of the A549 cells with different treatments were shown in Fig. 6 a and b, respectively. Compared to the control group, the fluorescence intensity of the 4 °C group decreased by ∼78%, indicating that the endocytosis pathway was energy-dependent. In terms of the inhibitor groups, MβCD, genistein, and amiloride could reduce the cellular uptake efficiency by around 50%, 21%, and 15%, respectively, while CPZ did not exert an inhibitory effect on the cellular uptake of the BNCDs. The above results suggested that the endocytosis pathways of BNCDs were energy-dependent and mainly involved LrME. Such an LrME-correlated LD targeting was also realized in a previous study via a quantum dot-based nanoprobe [64]. In this work, Mandal et al. demonstrated that the nanoprobe consisting of a quantum dot core and a polymer shell with anionic, cationic, and lipophilic functional groups could enter HeLa cells via LrME and selectively target LDs.

Owing to the poor water-dispersity of BNCDs, we also used amphiphilic polymers PF127 and DSPE-PEG2000-OMe (abbreviated as DPO) to encapsulate the BNCDs, and the resultant micellar assemblies were termed PF127/BNCDs and DPO/BNCDs, respectively (Fig. 7 a). The TEM images shown in Fig. S7 revealed that PF127/BNCDs and DPO/BNCDs had average sizes of ∼45 and ∼40 nm, respectively, indicating that they might be suitable for use in LD imaging. As expected, the confocal imaging results in Fig. 7b confirmed that the two micelles could realize intracellular LD staining with the imaging quality as high as that of BNCDs. The above results showed that BNCDs can be engineered as water-dispersible nanoprobes for LD imaging, which overcomes the relatively poor water-dispersity of free BNCDs and improves the application feasibility of the CDs.

LD is a highly dynamic organelle and tracing its movement is crucial for studying lipid metabolism. Accordingly, we further applied BNCDs to track the dynamic changes of LDs in the cytoplasm of A549 cells. We used four pseudo-colors to visualize the position changes of LDs. As shown in Fig. 8 , the trajectory of LDs in the selected cells can be clearly observed, validating the ability of BNCDs to dynamically track LDs and the potential of them to monitor lipid metabolism in cells.

Lipophagy is an important mode of intracellular lipid metabolism and related to many diseases like obesity and cardiovascular diseases. Thus, it is important to develop a method for tracking the process of lipophagy in live cells. Considering their good biocompatibility, high staining selectivity for LDs, and dynamic LD tracking capability, BNCDs may be suitable for monitoring lipophagy processes at the cellular level.

We firstly induced lipophagy in A549 cells by culturing the cells in a low-glucose medium, and then treated the cells with both BNCDs and LysoTracker Green. As shown in Fig. 9 , the red fluorescence of BNCDs and the green fluorescence of LysoTracker Green did not overlap at 15 min. When the incubation time increased to 1 h, partial overlap of the red and green dots (i.e., the formation of some yellow dots) was observed, which indicated the formation of autolysosomes. After 24 h, a significant increase of the autolysosomes could be observed, indicating the development of the degree of autophagy. Therefore, BNCDs can serve as a suitable probe for tracking the process of lipophagy in live cells.

In this work, using a facile dry heating approach, we developed orange/red dual-emissive CDs by employing 3-aminophenylboronic acid as the raw material. The obtained B- and N-codoped CDs (termed BNCDs) have the advantages of long emission wavelength, excitation-independent emission, good biocompatibility, and wash-free imaging capacity. Furthermore, the preparation approach of BNCDs was very simple and the raw material 3-aminophenylboronic acid was cheap and commercially available, making the CDs suitable for large-scale production and wide applications. When incubated with cells, the BNCDs could be rapidly endocytosed and be stably localized in LDs, ensuring dynamic monitoring of LDs. In addition, it was revealed that the BNCDs could also track the real-time lipophagy process in live cells. We believe that this CD-based nanoprobe will find applications in LDs-related biomedical research fields and promote the development of other subcellular structure-specific CD probes.

Cong She: Methodology, Investigation, Synthesis, Characterization, Analysis, Writing – original draft. Zihao Wang: Methodology, Investigation, Characterization, Analysis, Writing – original draft. Jia Zeng: Investigation, Characterization, Analysis. Fu-Gen Wu: Supervision, Methodology, Writing – original draft & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20211510) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21673037).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2022.02.005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Open questions in lipid droplet biology

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lipid droplets in inflammation and cancer

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dibenzothiophene core-based small-molecule AIE probe for wash-free and selective staining of lipid droplets in live mammalian and fungal cells

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A near-infrared AIEgen for specific imaging of lipid droplets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Kang, X. Gu, R.T.K. Kwok, C.W.T. Leung, J.W.Y. Lam, F. Li, B.Z. Tang, A near-infrared AIEgen for specific imaging of lipid droplets, Chem. Commun. 52 (2016) 5957-5960.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Specific fluorescence probes for lipid droplets based on simple AIEgens

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, C. Gui, E. Zhao, J. Wang, X. Li, A. Qin, Z. Zhao, Z. Yu, B.Z. Tang, Specific fluorescence probes for lipid droplets based on simple AIEgens, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 8 (2016) 10193−10200.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly selective AIE fluorogen for lipid droplet imaging in live cells and green algae

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A specific bioprobe for super-resolution fluorescence imaging of lipid droplets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zheng, W. Zhu, F. Ni, H. Ai, C. Yang, A specific bioprobe for super-resolution fluorescence imaging of lipid droplets, Sens. Actuators B: Chemical 255 (2018) 3148−3154.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous dual-colour tracking lipid droplets and lysosomes dynamics using a fluorescent probe

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solo smart fluorogenic probe for potential cancer diagnosis and tracking in vivo tumorous lymphatic systems via distinct emission signals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Samanta, M. Huang, F. Lin, P. Das, B. Chen, W. Yan, J.J. Chen, K. Ji, L. Liu, J. Qu, Z. Yang, Solo smart fluorogenic probe for potential cancer diagnosis and tracking in vivo tumorous lymphatic systems via distinct emission signals. Anal. Chem. 92 (2020) 1541−1548.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coumarin-based fluorescent probes for super-resolution and dynamic tracking of lipid droplets
